html:
Canto III

The Cocooning25

When the crop is fair in the olive-yard,
And the earthen jars are ready
For the golden oil from the barrels poured,
And the big cart rocks unsteady
With its tower of gathered sheaves, and strains
And groans on its way through fields and lanes;

When brawny and bare as an old athlete
Comes Bacchus the dance a-leading,
And the labourers all, with juice-dyed feet,
The vintage of Crau are treading,
And the good wine pours from the brimful presses,
And the ruddy foam in the vats increases;

When under the leaves of the Spanish broom
The clear silkworms are holden,
An artist each, in a tiny loom,
Weaving a web all goldenвҒ вҖ”
Fine, frail cells out of sunlight spun,
Where they creep and sleep by the millionвҒ вҖ”

Glad is Provence on a day like that,
вҖҷTis the time of jest and laughter:
The Ferigoulet26 and the Baume Muscat27
They quaff, and they sing thereafter.
And lads and lasses, their toils between,
Dance to the tinkling tambourine.

вҖңMethinks, good neighbours, I am FortuneвҖҷs pet.
NeвҖҷer in my trellised arbor saw I yet
A silkier bower, cocoons more worthy praise,
Or richer harvest, since the year of grace
When first I laid my hand on RamounвҖҷs arm
And came, a youthful bride, to Lotus Farm.вҖқ

So spake Jano Mario, RamounвҖҷs wife,
The fond, proud mother who had given life
To our MirГЁio. Unto her had hied,
The while were gathered the cocoons outside,
Her neighbours. In the silk-worm-room they throng;
And, as they aid the picking, gossip long.

To these MirГЁio tendered now and then
Oak-sprigs and sprays of rosemary; for when
The worms, lured by the mountain odour, come
In myriads, there to make their silken home,
The sprays and sprigs, adornГЁd in such wise,
Are like the golden palms of Paradise.

вҖңOn Mother MaryвҖҷs altar yesterday,вҖқ
Jano Mario said, вҖңI went to lay
My finer sprays, by way of tithe. And so
I do each year; for you, my women, know
That, when the holy Mother will, вҖҷtis she
Who sendeth up the worms abundantly.вҖқ

вҖңNow, for my part,вҖқ said ZГЁu of Host Farm,
вҖңGreat fears have I my worms will come to harm.
You mind that ugly day the east wind blewвҒ вҖ”
I left my window openвҒ вҖ”if you knew
Ever such folly!вҒ вҖ”and to my affright
Upon my floor are twenty, now turned white.вҖқ28

To ZГЁu thus the crone Taven repliedвҒ вҖ”
A witch, who from the cliffs of Baux had hied
To help at the cocooning: вҖңYouth is bold,
The young think they know better than the old;
And age is torment, and we mourn the fate
Which bids us see and knowвҒ вҖ”but all too late,

вҖңYe are such giddy women, every one,
That, if the hatching promise well, ye run
Straightway about the streets the tale to tell.
вҖҳCome see my silkworms! вҖҷTis incredible
How fine they are!вҖҷ Envy can well dissemble:
She hastens to your room, her heart a-tremble

вҖңWith wrath. And вҖҳWell done, neighbour!вҖҷ she says cheerly:
вҖҳThis does one good! YouвҖҷve still your caul29 on, clearly!вҖҷ
But when your head is turned, she casts upon вҖҷemвҒ вҖ”
The envious oneвҒ вҖ”a look so full of venom,
It knots and burns вҖҷem up. And then you say
It was the east wind plastered вҖҷem that way!вҖқ

вҖңI donвҖҷt say that has naught to do with it,вҖқ
Quoth ZГЁu. вҖңStill it had been quite as fit
For me to close the window.вҖқвҒ вҖ”вҖңDoubt you, then,
The harm the eye can do,вҖқ went on Taven,
вҖңWhen in the head it glistens balefully?вҖқ
And ZГЁu scanned, herself with piercing eye.

вҖңYe are such fools, ye seem to think,вҖқ she said,
вҖңThat scraping with a scalpel on the dead
Would win its honey-secret from the bee!
But may not a fierce look, now answer me,
The unborn babe for evermore deform,
And dry the cowвҖҷs milk in her udders warm?

вҖңAn owl may fascinate a little bird;
A serpent, flying geese, as I have heard,
How high soeвҖҷer they mount. And if one keep
A fixed gaze upon silkworms, will they sleep?
Moreover, is there, neighbours, in the land
So wise a virgin that she can withstand

вҖңThe fiery eyes of passionate youth?вҖқ Here stopped
The hag, and damsels four their cocoons dropped;
вҖңIn June as in October,вҖқ murmuring,
вҖңHer tongue hath evermore a barbГЁd sting,
The ancient viper! What the lads, say you?
Let them come, then! WeвҖҷll see what they can do?вҖқ

But other merry ones retorted, вҖңNo!
We want them not! Do we, MirГЁio?вҖқ
вҖңNot we! Nor is it always cocooning,
So IвҖҷll a bottle from the cellar bring
That you will find delicious.вҖқ And she fled
Toward the house because her cheeks grew red.

вҖңNow, friends,вҖқ said haughty Lauro, with decision,
вҖңThis is my mind, though poor be my condition:
IвҖҷll smile on no one, even though my lover
As king of fairy-land his realm should offer.
A pleasure were it, could I see him lying,
And seven long years before my footstool sighing.вҖқ

вҖңAh!вҖқ said ClemenГ§o, вҖңshould a king me woo,
And say he loved me, without much ado
IвҖҷd grant the royal suit! And chiefly thus
Were he a young king and a glorious.
A king of men, in beauty, IвҖҷd let come
And freely lead me to his palace home!

вҖңBut see! If I were once enthronГЁd there,
A sovereign and an empress, in a fair
Mantle bedecked, of golden-flowered brocade,
With pearls and emeralds dazzling round my head,
Then would my heart for my poor country yearn;
And I, the queen, would unto Baux return.

вҖңAnd I would make my capital at Baux,
And on the rock where lie its ruins low
I would rebuild our ancient castle, and
A white tower on the top thereof should stand
Whose head should touch the stars. Thither retiring,
If rest or solace were the queen desiring,

вҖңWeвҖҷd climb the turret-stair, my prince and I,
And gladly throw the crown and mantle by.
And would it not be blissful with my love,
Aloft, alone to sit, the world above?
Or, leaned upon the parapet by his side,
To search the lovely landscape far and wide,

вҖңOur own glad kingdom of Provence descrying,
Like some great orange-grove beneath us lying
All fair? And, ever stretching dreamily
Beyond the hills and plains, the sapphire sea;
While noble ships, tricked out with streamers gay,
Just graze the ChГўteau dвҖҷIf, and pass away?

вҖңOr we would turn to lightning-scathed Ventour,30
Who, while the lesser heights before him cower,
His hoary head against the heaven raises,
As I have seen, in solitary places
Of beech and pine, with staff in agГЁd hand,
Some shepherd-chief, his flock oвҖҷerlooking, stand.

вҖңAgain, weвҖҷd follow the great RhГҙne awhile,
Adown whose banks the cities brave defile,
And dip their lips and drink, with dance and song.
Stately is the RhГҙneвҖҷs march, and very strong;
But even he must bend at Avignon
His haughty head to Notre Dame des Doms.31

вҖңOr watch the ever-varying Durance,
Now like some fierce and ravenous goat advance
Devouring banks and bridges; now demure
As maid from rustic well who bears her ewer,
Spilling her scanty water as she dallies,
And every youth along her pathway rallies.вҖқ

So spake her sweet ProvenГ§al majesty,
And rose with brimful apron, and put by
Her gathered treasure. Two more maids were there,
Twin sisters, the one dark, the other fairвҒ вҖ”
AzalГӨis, ViГІulano. The stronghold
Of Estoublon sheltered their parents old.

And oft these two to Lotus Farmstead came;
While that mischievous lad, Cupid by name,
Who loves to sport with generous hearts and tender,
Had made the sisters both their love surrender
To the same youth. So AzalГӨis saidвҒ вҖ”
The dark oneвҒ вҖ”lifting up her raven head:

вҖңNow, damsels, play awhile that I were queen.
The Marseilles ships, the Beaucaire meadows green.
Smiling La Ciotat, and fair Salon,
With all her almond trees, to me belong.
Then the young maids IвҖҷd summon by decree,
From Arles, Baux, Barbentano, unto me.

вҖңвҖҠвҖҳCome, fly like birds!вҖҷ the order should be given;
And I, of these, would choose the fairest seven,
And royal charge upon the same would lay,
The false love and the true in scales to weigh.
And then would merry counsel holden be;
For sure it is a great calamity

вҖңThat half of those who love, with love most meet,
Can never marry, and their joy complete.
But when I, AzalГӨis, hold the helm,
I proclamation make, that in my realm
True lovers wounded in their cruel sport
Shall aye find mercy at the maidenвҖҷs court.

вҖңAnd if one sell her robe of honour white,
Whether it be for gold or jewel bright,
And if one offer insult, or betray
A fond heart, unto such as these alway
The high court of the seven maids shall prove
The stern avenger of offended love.

вҖңAnd if two lovers the same maid desire,
Or if two maids to the same lad aspire,
My councilвҖҷs duty it shall be to choose
Which loves the better, which the better sues,
And which is worthier of a happy fate.
Moreover, on my maidens there shall wait

вҖңSeven sweet poets, who from time to time
Shall write the laws of love in lovely rhyme
Upon wild vine-leaves or the bark of trees;
And sometimes, in a stately chorus, these
Will sing the same, and then their couplets all
Like honey from the honey-comb will fall.вҖқ

So, long ago, the whispering pines among,
Faneto de GautГЁume32 may have sung,
When she the glory of her star-crowned head
On Roumanin and on the Alpines shed;
Or Countess Dio,33 of the passionate lays,
Who held her courts of love in the old days.

But now MirГЁio, to the room returning,
With face as radiant as an Easter morning,
A flagon bore; and, for their spiritsвҖҷ sake,
Besought them all her beverage to partake:
вҖңFor this will make us work with heartier will;
So come, good women, and your goblets fill!вҖқ

Then, pouring from the wicker-covered flask
A generous drink for whosoeвҖҷer might ask,
(A string of gold the falling liquor made),
вҖңI mixed this cordial mine own self,вҖқ she said:
вҖңOne leaves it in a window forty days,
That it may mellow in the sunвҖҷs hot rays.

вҖңHerein are mountain herbs, in number three.
The liquor keeps their odour perfectly:
It strengthens one.вҖқ Here brake in other voices:
вҖңListen, MirГЁio! Tell us what your choice is;
For these have told what they would do, if they
Were queens, or came to great estate one day.

вҖңIn such a case, MirГЁio, what would you?вҖқ
вҖңWho, I? How can I tell what I would do?
I am so happy in our own La Crau
With my dear parents, wherefore should I go?вҖқ
вҖңAh, ha!вҖқ outspake another maiden bold:
вҖңLittle care you for silver or for gold.

вҖңBut on a certain morn, I mind it wellвҒ вҖ”
Forgive me, dear, that I the tale should tell!вҒ вҖ”
вҖҷTwas Tuesday: I had gathered sticks that day,
And, fagot on my hip, had won my way
Almost to La Crous-Blanco, when I вҖҷspied
You in a tree, with some one by your side

вҖңWho chatted gayly. A lithe form he hadвҖқвҒ вҖ”
вҖңWhence did he come?вҖқ they cried. вҖңWho was the lad?вҖқ
Said Noro, вҖңTo tell that were not so easy,
Because among the thick-leaved mulberry-trees he
Was hidden half; yet think I вҖҷtwas the clever
Vincen, the Valabregan basket-weaver!вҖқ

вҖңOh!вҖқ cried the damsels all, with peals of laughter,
вҖңSee you not what the little cheat was after?
A pretty basket she would fain receive,
And made this poor boy in her love believe!
The fairest maiden the whole country over
Has chosen the barefoot Vincen for her lover!вҖқ

So mocked they, till oвҖҷer each young countenance
In turn there fell a dark and sidelong glanceвҒ вҖ”
TavenвҖҷsвҒ вҖ”who cried, вҖңA thousand curses fall
Upon you, and the vampire34 seize you all!
If the good Lord from heaven this way came,
You girls, I think, would giggle all the same.

вҖңвҖҠвҖҷTis brave to laugh at this poor lad of osiers;
But mark! the future may make strange disclosures,
Poor though he be. Now hear the oracle!
God in his house once wrought a miracle;
And I can show the truth of what I say,
For, lasses, it all happened in my day.

вҖңOnce, in the wild woods of the Luberon,35
A shepherd kept his flock. His days were long;
But when at last the same were well-nigh spent,
And toward the grave his iron frame was bent,
He sought the hermit of Saint OuquГЁri,
To make his last confession piously.

вҖңAlone, in the Vaumasco36 valley lost,
His foot had never sacred threshold crost,
Since he partook his first communion.
Even his prayers were from his memory gone;
But now he rose and left his cottage lowly,
And came and bowed before the hermit holy.

вҖңвҖҠвҖҳWith what sin chargest thou thyself, my brother?вҖҷ
The solitary said. Replied the other,
The aged man, вҖҳOnce, long ago, I slew
A little bird about my flock that flewвҒ вҖ”
A cruel stone I flung its life to end:
It was a wagtail, and the shepherdsвҖҷ friend.вҖҷ

вҖңвҖҠвҖҳIs this a simple soul,вҖҷ the hermit thought,
вҖҳOr is it an impostor?вҖҷ And he sought
Right curiously to read the old manвҖҷs face
Until, to solve the riddle, вҖҳGo,вҖҷ he says,
вҖҳAnd hang thy shepherdвҖҷs cloak yon beam upon,
And afterward I will absolve my son.вҖҷ

вҖңA single sunbeam through the chapel strayed;
And there it was the priest the suppliant bade
To hang his cloak! But the good soul arose,
And drew it off with mien of all repose,
And threw it upward. And it hung in sight
Suspended on the slender shaft of light!

вҖңThen fell the hermit prostrate on the floor,
вҖҳOh, man of God!вҖҷ he cried, and he wept sore,
вҖҳLet but the blessed hand these tears bedew,
Fulfil the sacred office for us two!
No sins of thine can I absolve, вҖҷtis clear:
Thou art the saint, and I the sinner here!вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ

Her story ended, the crone said no more;
But all the laughter of the maids was oвҖҷer.
Only Laureto dared one little joke:
вҖңThis tells us neвҖҷer to laugh at any cloak!
Good may the beast be, although rough the hide;
But, girls, methought young mistress I espied

вҖңGrow crimson as an autumn grape, because
VincenвҖҷs dear name so lightly uttered was.
ThereвҖҷs mystery here! MirГЁio, we are jealous!
Lasted the picking long that day? Pray, tell us!
When two friends meet, the hour is winged with pleasure;
And, for a lover, one has always leisure!вҖқ

вҖңOh, fie!вҖқ MirГЁio said. вҖңEnough of joking!
Mind your work now, and be not so provoking!
You would make swear the very saints! But I
Promise you one and all, most faithfully,
IвҖҷll seek a convent while my years are tender,
Sooner than eвҖҷer my maiden heart surrender!вҖқ

Then brake the damsels into merry chorus:
вҖңHave we not pretty Magali before us?
Who love and lovers held in such disdain
That, to escape their torment, she was fain
To Saint BlasiвҖҷs in Arles away to hie,
And bury her sweet self from every eye.вҖқ

вҖңCome, Noro, you, whose voice is ever thrilling,
Who charm us all, sing now, if you are willing,
The song of Magali, the cunning fairy,
Who love had shunned by all devices airy.
A bird, a vine, a sunbeam she became,
Yet fell herself, loveвҖҷs victim all the same!

вҖңQueen of my soul!вҖқ sang Noro, and the rest
Fell straightway to their work with twofold zest;
And as, when one cicala doth begin
Its high midsummer note, the rest fall in
And swell the chorus, so the damsels here
Sang the refrain with voices loud and clear:вҒ вҖ”

I37

вҖңMagali, queen of my soul,
The dawn is near!
Hark to my tambourine,
Hide not thy bower within,
Open and hear!

II

вҖңThe sky is full of stars,
And the wind soft;
But, when thine eyes they see,
The stars, O Magali,
Will pale aloft!вҖқ

III

вҖңIdle as summer breeze
The tune thou playest!
IвҖҷll vanish in the sea,
A silver eel will be,
Ere thou me stayest.вҖқ

IV

вҖңIf thou become an eel,
And so forsake me,
I will turn fisher too
And fish the water blue
Until I take thee!вҖқ

V

вҖңIn vain with net or line
Thou me implorest:
IвҖҷll be a bird that day,
And wing my trackless way
Into the forest!вҖқ

VI

вҖңIf thou become a bird,
And so dost dare me,
I will a fowler be,
And follow cunningly
Until I snare thee!вҖқ

VII

вҖңWhen thou thy cruel snare
Settest full surely,
I will a flower become,
And in my prairie home
Hide me securely!вҖқ

VIII

вҖңIf thou become a flower,
Before thou thinkest
IвҖҷll be a streamlet clear,
And all the water bear
That thou, love, drinkest!вҖқ

IX

вҖңWhen thou, a stream, dost feed
The flower yonder,
I will turn cloud straightway,
And to America
Away IвҖҷll wander.вҖқ

X

вҖңThough thou to India
Fly from thy lover,
Still I will follow thee:
I the sea-breeze will be
To waft thee over!вҖқ

XI

вҖңI can outstrip the breeze
Fast as it flieth:
IвҖҷll be the swift sun-ray
That melts the ice away
And the grass drieth!вҖқ

XII

вҖңSunlight if thou become,
Are my wiles ended?
IвҖҷll be a lizard green,
And quaff the golden sheen
To make me splendid!вҖқ

XIII

вҖңBe thou a Triton, hid
In the dark sedges!
IвҖҷm the moon by whose ray
Fairies and witches pay
Their mystic pledges!вҖқ

XIV

вҖңIf thou the moon wilt be
Sailing in glory,
IвҖҷll be the halo white
Hovering every night
Around and oвҖҷer thee!вҖқ

XV

вҖңYet shall thy shadowy arm
Embrace me never!
I will turn virgin rose,
And all my thorns oppose
To thee for ever!вҖқ

XVI

вҖңIf thou become a rose,
Vain too shall this be!
Seest thou not that I,
As a bright butterfly,
Freely may kiss thee?вҖқ

XVII

вҖңUrge, then, thy mad pursuit:
Idly thouвҖҷlt follow!
IвҖҷll in the deep wood bide;
IвҖҷll in the old oak hide,
GnarlГЁd and hollow.вҖқ

XVIII

вҖңIn the dim forest glade
Wilt thou be hidden?
IвҖҷll be the ivy-vine,
And my long arms entwine
Round thee unbidden!вҖқ

XIX

вҖңFold thine arms tightly, then:
Clasp the oak only!
IвҖҷll a white sister be!
Far off in St.В Blasi,
Secure and lonely!вҖқ

XX

вҖңBe thou a white-veiled nun
Come to confession,
I will be there as priest,
Thee freely to divest
Of all transgression!вҖқ

The startled women their cocoons let fall.
вҖңNoro, make haste!вҖқ outspake they one and all:
вҖңWhat could our hunted Magali answer then?
A nun, poor dear, who had already been
A cloud, a bird, a fish, an oak, a flower,
The sun, the moon, the stream, in one short hour?вҖқ

вҖңAh, yes!вҖқ said Noro, вҖңI the rest will sing:
She was, I think, the cloister entering;
And that mad fowler dared to promise her
He would in the confessional appear,
And shrive her. Therefore hear what she replies:
The maid hath yet another last device:вҖқвҒ вҖ”

XXI

вҖңEnter the sacred house!
I shall be sleeping,
Robed in a winding-sheet,
Nuns at my head and feet,
Above me weeping.вҖқ

XXII

вҖңIf thou wert lifeless dust,
My toils were oвҖҷer:
IвҖҷd be the yawning grave,
Thee in my arms to have
For evermore!вҖқ

XXIII

вҖңNow know I thou art true,
Leave me not yet!
Come, singer fair, and take,
And wear it for my sake,
This annulet!вҖқ

XXIV

вҖңLook up, my blessed one,
The heaven scan!
Since the stars came to see
Thee, O my Magali,
They are turned wan!вҖқ

A silence fell, the sweet song being ended:
Only with the last moving notes had blended
The voices of the rest. Their heads were drooping,
As they before the melody were stooping,
Like slender reeds that lean and sway for ever
Before the flowing eddies of a river.

Till Noro said, вҖңNow is the air serene;
And here the mowers come, their scythes to clean
Beside the vivary brook. MirГЁio, dear,
Bring us a few St.В JohnвҖҷs Day apples here.
And we will add a little new-made cheese,
And take our lunch beneath the lotus-trees.вҖқ
